intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

body.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

draft.title: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

Building your first draft…

data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

facade.hypothesis: Users tracking health experiments want visual trends at a glance, not dense tables—compact metric cards with embedded mini-charts beat scrollable data.
facade.label: Number cards with sparklines
facade.acceptImplies: Dashboard uses card-based metric display with embedded charts; favors visual scanning over tabular lookup.
facade.rejectImplies: Dashboard uses tables, lists, or full-size charts; users prefer detailed breakdowns or explicit number hierarchies over summarized visuals.

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer conversational, frequent micro-interactions over clinical data entry for health tracking
facade.label: Casual check-in style
facade.acceptImplies: Design around brief, chat-like logging (e.g., 'How'd you sleep?' instead of 'Sleep Duration: __ hours'). Use warm, encouragement-focused copy.
facade.rejectImplies: Lean toward structured, clinical interfaces with standardized forms and neutral tone. Prioritize data integrity over engagement feel.

evidence.hypothesis: Users tracking health experiments want visual trends at a glance, not dense tables—compact metric cards with embedded mini-charts beat scrollable data.
evidence.implication: Dashboard uses card-based metric display with embedded charts; favors visual scanning over tabular lookup.

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer a persistent left sidebar for experiment navigation paired with spacious card-based layouts for viewing health metrics, reducing cognitive load from tab switching
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation pattern (sidebar vs tabs) × Information density (cards vs lists)
facade.label: Sidebar with cards
facade.acceptImplies: Prioritize stable, always-visible navigation for quick experiment context-switching; card-based summary view works better than dense tabular lists for health dashboards
facade.rejectImplies: Explore tab-based or tab-like navigation; users may prefer more compact list layouts or in-context navigation that doesn't demand persistent screen real estate

facade.hypothesis: Users building health dashboards prefer clean, restrained data visualization that feels calm and analytical rather than colorful or decorative
facade.axisTargeted: Organic vs digital—specifically whether visual simplicity and restraint (closer to analog/minimal) or richer graphical density feels right
facade.label: Minimal line charts
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into understated, line-based visuals. Single-color palettes. White space. Data as quiet story, not visual spectacle.
facade.rejectImplies: Users want more visual warmth, texture, or richness. Consider softer fills, color gradients, or more tactile/organic rendering of the data itself.

facade.hypothesis: A health dashboard with high contrast dark mode and glowing data points will feel more clinical and precise, supporting focus on detailed tracking
facade.axisTargeted: Light vs dark—specifically whether the interface should use dark backgrounds or light backgrounds as its foundation
facade.label: Dark cards with light text
facade.acceptImplies: User leans toward dark, high-contrast interfaces; prefers a more tech-forward, serious aesthetic for health data
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers lighter, airier interfaces; may want a softer, more approachable feeling for personal health tracking despite the analytical nature

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights about patterns
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations that encourage reflection

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights about patterns
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations that encourage reflection
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: A health dashboard benefits from fluid animations between states rather than instant changes—making data shifts feel like natural progression rather than jarring updates.
facade.axisTargeted: Animated vs Static — do micro-interactions and state changes use motion or happen instantly?
facade.label: Smooth transitions
facade.acceptImplies: User values visual continuity and ease of following changes. Likely wants animations throughout (scrolling, value updates, section reveals). Expect preference for gentle easing and longer feedback loops.
facade.rejectImplies: User favors directness and speed. Likely wants instant load and minimal animation overhead. Expects snappy interactions, instant number updates, minimal visual polish. May find motion distracting or frivolous.

facade.hypothesis: Users tracking health experiments prefer animated metric updates and smooth visual transitions between states over instant, jarring number changes—motion makes data feel alive and less clinical.
facade.axisTargeted: Motion and temporal feedback in health dashboards
facade.label: Soft data transitions
facade.acceptImplies: Dashboard should use gentle animations, easing functions, and progressive reveals when metrics update; favors living, breathing interface over static snapshot.
facade.rejectImplies: Dashboard updates instantaneously without animation; metrics feel static and transactional; motion adds friction rather than clarity.
